video_title: Snowflake CEO: How AI Agents Will Transform the Workplace | Podcast | In Good Company
gist: Snowflake CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy tells NBIM's Nicolai Tangen that AI coding agents and the model labs (Anthropic et al.) — not Amazon or Microsoft — are now Snowflake's biggest competitive threat because they are industrializing software and becoming the 'front door' to computing. He details how AI is collapsing data-engineering costs, why Snowflake's consumption pricing is advantaged in an AI world, and how agentic interfaces are reshaping enterprise data work.

themes.theme: Coding agents as Snowflake's primary competitive threat

insights.claim: Snowflake CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy now considers AI model companies like Anthropic and their coding agents to be Snowflake's biggest competition, not traditional database vendors, because they are industrializing software and becoming the 'front door' to all computing.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Reframes Snowflake's competitive set toward foundation-model labs; investors should watch whether Snowflake's interface and agent layer can defend against model companies owning the user entry point.
insights.quote: I think of uh coding agents as the biggest threat to all software and uh figuring out a path for snowflake that is going to survive and thrive in this world is my number one challenge.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy argues that AI models represent the industrialization of software at a scale not seen before, ending the era where software was a craft produced by scarce expert programmers comparable to concert pianists.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Implies structural deflation in software production costs, pressuring the margins and moats of incumbent software vendors that rely on labor-intensive development.
insights.quote: what these models represent with software is the industrialization of software at a scale that we have not seen before

insights.claim: Snowflake built its own coding agent (Cortex Code) specifically as a defensive response to coding agents being the 'front door' to computing, starting with software engineers and expanding to everyone.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Cortex Code adoption and 'love' are a leading indicator of whether Snowflake can hold the user entry point against model labs.
insights.quote: This is the reason why we have a coding agent. They represent the front door to computing, the front door to all information more and more for everybody.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy explicitly downplays Amazon and Microsoft as competitors in pure data platforms, saying Snowflake has 'always been top of the charts,' while warning hyperscalers should never be underestimated given their resources.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals where management sees its durable advantage (data platform) versus its vulnerability (agent/model layer), useful for assessing competitive positioning.

themes.theme: Consumption pricing advantaged in the AI era

insights.claim: Snowflake's consumption-based pricing — charging for what customers actually use rather than per seat — becomes a bigger advantage in the AI world because customers only pay for what they use and revenue is recognized on actual usage.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Consumption pricing aligns Snowflake's revenue with AI-driven usage spikes; investors should track consumption growth as the key revenue lever rather than seat counts.
insights.quote: in the world of AI that's a big deal because you only pay for what you use

insights.claim: Snowflake leverages over 13,000 customers to predict and amortize spiky, bursty demand across its base, allowing it to offer a more stable, cost-effective price without requiring customers to commit to fixed storage or CPU.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Scale-driven demand amortization is a moat that improves as the customer base grows, supporting pricing power and gross margin.
insights.quote: we have over 13,000 customers and uh so we are able to amatize spiky demand ourselves

insights.claim: Snowflake's separation of compute from storage lets customers spin up a thousand computers for a weekend analytic job and shut them down afterward, paying only for that burst of usage.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: The compute/storage separation is the architectural basis for Snowflake's elasticity and consumption model that competitors must match.
insights.quote: You can spin up a thousand computers over one weekend do the analysis shut them down

themes.theme: AI collapsing data-engineering costs and timelines

insights.claim: AI coding agents are collapsing data-engineering tasks that once took quarters or years, with Snowflake working on agent-driven migrations that move data from legacy systems onto Snowflake in days to a small number of weeks.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Dramatically faster migrations could accelerate Snowflake's customer onboarding and land-and-expand, shortening sales cycles and reducing a major adoption barrier.
insights.quote: you can move data from legacy systems onto snowflake in a matter of days and small number of weeks as opposed to the multiple quarters in the multiple years that it used to take

insights.claim: Snowflake's 'skills' — described as English-language programs — automate data-pipeline changes like adding a column that previously took a programmer a full week, completing them in about an hour.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Order-of-magnitude productivity gains in data engineering reduce the cost of cleaning and maintaining data, lowering the friction that historically limited AI value extraction.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy says messy data with duplicates, gaps and legacy systems used to be a huge barrier to getting value from data but is getting simpler by the day thanks to coding agents applied to the data problem itself.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: If data-cleanup costs fall sharply, the addressable value of analytics platforms expands and prior data-debt becomes less of a constraint on enterprise AI rollouts.
insights.quote: Used to be a huge barrier to getting value from data. I think it is getting simpler by the day. That's the magic of uh coding agents applied to the data problem itself.

themes.theme: Agentic interfaces reshaping enterprise data work

insights.claim: Snowflake Intelligence provides an agentic natural-language interface to enterprise data, letting non-technical users ask questions like why an investment declined and get analyst-grade breakdowns without human analysts.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Agentic data interfaces could expand Snowflake's user base beyond data engineers to business users, increasing query volume and consumption revenue.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy frames coding agents as 'abstraction agents' that benefit even people who never write code, giving them programmable access to all documents and structured data so any data question becomes a plan the agent executes.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Broadens the agent total addressable market from developers to all knowledge workers, supporting a larger usage base for data platforms.
insights.quote: These coding agents are effectively abstraction agents.

insights.claim: Snowflake emphasizes interoperability via MCP (model context protocol), letting every agent created in Snowflake be called remotely from a customer's own front-end interface.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: An interoperability strategy hedges against losing the front door, positioning Snowflake as the data backend regardless of which agent customers use.
insights.quote: every agent that is created in Snowflake can be called remotely from some front door that um you know that you have and that's what MCP is. It is an interoperability layer.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy predicts agents will make everything programmable and interconnected, so workers can trigger actions like sending an email directly from an analysis without cutting and pasting between systems.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Cross-system action-taking by agents threatens the seams between SaaS tools and could consolidate workflows onto whichever platform owns the agent layer.
insights.quote: Everything is programmable and also everything is interconnected.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy says software engineering is nothing like it was two years ago, with Snowflake pushing 'spec-driven' development where engineers write an English-language spec and automate the writing, testing and deployment of the first code version.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: A shift to spec-driven coding implies large engineering productivity gains and potential headcount leverage across the software industry.
insights.quote: software engineer is software engineering is nothing like what it was two years ago

insights.claim: Ramaswamy claims Snowflake's superstar engineers are now 50 to 100 times more productive than the average software engineer when leveraging AI tools.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: If top-engineer productivity multiples are real, it widens talent dispersion and reshapes how software firms staff and value engineering teams.
insights.quote: we have the superstars, people that are 50, 100 times more productive than the average software institute

insights.claim: Ramaswamy says the modern AI-driven software engineer is more conceptual and effectively manages a team of agents, exercising taste and judgment rather than driving low-level coding details.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Signals which engineering skills retain value (judgment, problem selection) versus those being automated, relevant for tech labor and edtech theses.
insights.quote: They are actually managing a team of agents.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy recounts that his 24-year-old son, a recently graduated systems programmer specializing in low-latency streaming, told him everything he learned in school is useless at his current AI-lab job, illustrating how fast the discipline is changing.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Underscores rapid obsolescence of traditional CS training, with implications for tech-talent pipelines and education providers.
insights.quote: Dad, everything I learned in school and after that is completely useless to succeed in my current job.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy argues GDPR ultimately benefited large incumbents who could afford compliance while raising the cost of doing business for every European company and burdening new startups out of the gate.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Supports a thesis that heavy regulation entrenches scale players; relevant for European tech competitiveness and where new entrants can emerge.
insights.quote: the people that ultimately benefited from GDPR were the giants who are able to spend the money, follow all the all the rules and be compliant while every new company that comes up in Europe has to struggle

insights.claim: Ramaswamy credits Snowflake's governance features as a selling point because GDPR compliance is a burden for companies, positioning Snowflake to capture the cost-of-compliance need.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Regulatory complexity can be a demand driver for Snowflake's governance tooling, a defensible value-add in regulated industries.

themes.theme: Leadership, turnaround and the CEO operating model

insights.claim: Ramaswamy became Snowflake CEO at a critical juncture when growth was slowing, and on his first day the company guided five full percentage points below consensus for the upcoming year, which he says was the real reason for the share-price drop.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Clarifies that the leadership-transition stock drop was driven by guidance, not the CEO change itself — useful context for interpreting the turnaround narrative.
insights.quote: we guided five full percentage points below consensus for the year uh for the upcoming year. That was the u real reason for the dramatic drop.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy runs 'war rooms' that reorganize teams vertically around a new product area with daily and weekly feedback loops, planning on Monday and demanding results by Friday to drive rapid AI-era execution.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: A concrete operating cadence signaling how Snowflake is compressing product cycles, a qualitative read on execution speed for investors.
insights.quote: we plan on Monday and in the world of AI I want to see results on Friday

insights.claim: Ramaswamy says Snowflake's turnaround was driven by a singular focus on creating products people love rather than other management levers, citing Cortex Code as the result.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Product-led strategy framing; Cortex Code adoption becomes the proof point investors can track for the turnaround thesis.
insights.quote: at the end of the day if you are in software life is fundamentally about are you creating products that people love

themes.theme: NBIM's Snowflake footprint and founder lessons

insights.claim: NBIM, the Norwegian sovereign wealth fund, is both an investor in and heavy user of Snowflake, storing two petabytes of data and running roughly 3 million queries per day on the platform.
insights.speaker: Nicolai Tangen (NBIM CEO, host)
insights.so_what: A concrete reference-customer datapoint at sovereign-fund scale evidences Snowflake's mission-critical role in demanding institutional workloads.
insights.quote: We have two pabytes of data in Snowflake which is the equivalent of 2 million gigabytes and we have roughly 3 million queries into the database every day.

insights.claim: Ramaswamy says his failed search startup Neeva failed because a consumer product needs a dramatically better experience to win adoption, and a merely marginally better, more private search engine was not enough.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: A reusable lesson that incremental improvement loses in consumer markets, relevant for evaluating new entrants against entrenched incumbents.
insights.quote: consumer products especially do not get adopted without a without an experience that is dramatically better

insights.claim: Ramaswamy notes that Neeva's technology and team became the underpinnings of what AI became at Snowflake, so the failed startup ultimately fed Snowflake's AI capabilities.
insights.speaker: Sridhar Ramaswamy (Snowflake CEO)
insights.so_what: Explains the origin of Snowflake's AI talent and technology, context for assessing the credibility of its AI roadmap.
insights.quote: it eventually ended up being the underpinnings of what AI became at Snowflake
